:::{#lem-ph-single-subj-fail}
#### Single-subject failure probability in a risk set

For any subject $j \in R(t_i)$, under noninformative censoring,
the probability that $j$ fails in the interval at $t_i$ given that $j$ is at risk
is the interval failure probability, and to first order in $\Delta t$,

$$
\ba
\Pr\sb{j \text{ fails at } t_i \mid j \in R(t_i)}
&= q_j(t_i)
&& \text{(definition of } q_j(t_i) \text{)}
\\
&\approx q^*_j(t_i)
&& \text{(first-order hazard approximation)}
\\
&= \blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\;\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)} .
&& \text{(definition of } q^*_j(t_i)\text{)}
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-single-subj}
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

::: {.proof}
Since $j \in R(t_i)$ means $Y_j = \min(T_j, C_j) \ge t_i$,
we have both $T_j \ge t_i$ and $C_j \ge t_i$.
Under noninformative censoring, the event $C_j \ge t_i$
carries no information about $T_j$ given $\vx_j$,
so conditioning on $j \in R(t_i)$ is equivalent to conditioning on $T_j \ge t_i$ alone.
This conditional failure probability is exactly the
[interval failure probability](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-interval-fail-prob)
$q_j(t_i)$.
The [first-order interval failure probability](intro-to-survival-analysis.qmd#def-first-order-interval-fail-prob)
then gives $q_j(t_i) \approx q^*_j(t_i)$,
and the proportional-hazards decomposition (@def-ph-first-order-interval-fail-prob)
splits $q^*_j(t_i)$ into the shared baseline factor $\blue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}$
and the subject-specific hazard ratio $\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}$.
:::

:::{#exm-ph-single-subj-fail}
#### Failure probabilities in a three-subject risk set

This example introduces a running scenario reused later in this section:
the risk set $R(t_i) = \set{1, 2, 3}$ at $t_i = 4$ yr,
with hazard ratios $\red{\hazfactor(\vx_1)} = 1$, $\red{\hazfactor(\vx_2)} = 2$, $\red{\hazfactor(\vx_3)} = 3$,
baseline $\blue{\haz_0(4)} = 0.1$/yr, and grid width $\Delta t = 0.5$ yr,
so the shared factor is $\blue{\haz_0(4)\,\Delta t} = 0.05$.
Subject $2$ is the one who fails at $t_i$.
By @eq-ph-single-subj,

$$
q^*_1(4) = 0.05,
\qquad
q^*_2(4) = 0.10,
\qquad
q^*_3(4) = 0.15 ,
$$

each the shared $0.05$ scaled by that subject's hazard ratio $\red{\hazfactor(\vx_j)}$.
:::
